claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's get started. To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to understand your situation.

**First, let me ask about your child:**

Do you have a child (or children) you're responsible for? If yes, how old is your child? (If you have multiple children, we can go through them one at a time.)

The age is important because:
- Children under 16 may automatically qualify
- Young people aged 16-19 may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Those aged 20 or over generally don't qualify

Please tell me about your child's age, and we'll proceed from there.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, yes, I'm asking about my son Alex. He's 8 years old and lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! Let me assess the situation for your son Alex who is 8 years old.

Since Alex is under 16, he falls into the automatic qualification age category. Now I need to check an important requirement - that he's not working full-time.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now, since Alex is 8 years old, I need to confirm:

**Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?**

Given that Alex is only 8 years old, I assume he's not in paid employment, but I need to confirm this to proceed with the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he doesn't work at all. He's just in primary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent, that's what I expected. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now I need to check another requirement:

**Is Alex NOT receiving certain benefits in his own right?**

This means - is Alex receiving Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit in his own name? 

For an 8-year-old in primary school, this would be very unusual, but I need to confirm that he's not receiving these types of benefits independently.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he doesn't receive any benefits in his own name. He's just a regular 8-year-old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! The next check is about apprenticeships:

**Is Alex in any type of apprenticeship program?**

Again, since Alex is only 8 years old and in primary school, I'm confident he's not in an apprenticeship, but I need to confirm this to move forward with the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, of course not! He's only 8, he's in primary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Absolutely right! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Now we need to check your residency status. This is an important requirement for Child Benefit.

**Do you meet the UK residency requirements?**

You can qualify if ANY of the following applies to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK** (this is the most common path)
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have pre-settled status and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold)
4. You have pre-settled status and are actively seeking work (up to 91 days)
5. You have pre-settled status with sufficient resources to support yourself
6. You have pre-settled status and are studying with sufficient resources
7. You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national with right to reside
8. You're living abroad as a Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

Which of these applies to you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester. I've lived here all my life.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! That satisfies the residency requirement. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check if you're responsible for Alex.

**Are you responsible for Alex?**

You mentioned earlier that Alex lives at home with you in Manchester, which means you satisfy the "living with the child" requirement. This is one of the main ways to demonstrate responsibility.

Just to confirm - Alex does live with you full-time (or most of the time), correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right. He lives with me full-time at home.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Now I need to ask an important question:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

For example:
- Alex's other parent
- Another family member or guardian
- Anyone else

Only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child at any given time. If someone else is claiming, they would need to agree to transfer the claim to you, or HMRC would need to decide who should receive it.

Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming for him.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now I need to check Alex's living situation. You mentioned he lives at home with you, but I need to confirm there are no special circumstances:

**Which of the following applies to Alex's current living situation?**

- Is he currently in hospital?
- Is he in local authority care?
- Is he in a foster care arrangement?
- Or is he living normally at home with you?

Based on what you've told me, it sounds like he's living normally at home with you, but I need to confirm this.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He's just living normally at home with me. Nothing special like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Just one final question:

**Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words - is the local council paying anything towards the cost of Alex's care, accommodation, or upkeep? 

Generally, you cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child's care. But since Alex lives at home with you and you're supporting him, I expect the answer is that the council is not paying, correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right, the council isn't paying anything. I'm supporting him myself.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## 🎉 Excellent News! You Are ELIGIBLE for Child Benefit!

Based on the information you've provided, **you meet all the eligibility requirements for Child Benefit for your son Alex.**

### Why You're Eligible:

✅ **Alex qualifies as a child** - He's 8 years old (under 16)  
✅ **Not working** - He's in primary school, not in paid employment  
✅ **Not receiving benefits** - He's not claiming benefits in his own right  
✅ **Not in an apprenticeship** - N/A for his age  
✅ **You meet residency requirements** - You live in the UK (Manchester)  
✅ **You're responsible for Alex** - He lives with you full-time  
✅ **No competing claims** - Nobody else is claiming for Alex  
✅ **Normal living arrangement** - Alex lives at home with you  
✅ **No council support** - The council isn't paying for his care  

### Next Steps to Claim:

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** or adoption certificate
4. **Be aware:** If you or your partner earn over £60,000, you may need to pay the High Income Child Benefit Charge (but you can still claim the benefit)
5. **Payment schedule:** Benefits are typically paid every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

### How to Apply:
Visit: https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

Congratulations, and please don't hesitate if you have any other questions!

reasonings: Alex is eligible for Child Benefit because: (1) He is 8 years old, which is under the age 16 threshold for automatic qualification. (2) He is not in paid work (attending primary school). (3) He is not receiving benefits in his own right such as ESA or Universal Credit. (4) He is not in any apprenticeship program. (5) The claimant normally lives in the UK (Manchester), satisfying residency requirements. (6) The claimant lives with Alex full-time, establishing responsibility for the child. (7) No one else is currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex. (8) Alex lives normally at home and is not in hospital, care, or foster arrangements. (9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria have been met.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their 8-year-old son Alex. The claimant meets all UK residency requirements (living in Manchester), has responsibility for Alex (living together full-time), and Alex meets all child eligibility criteria as an 8-year-old in primary school with no disqualifying circumstances. No other person is claiming for Alex, and the local council is not contributing to his care.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements

Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

(2) He is not in paid work (attending primary school). (3) He is not receiving benefits in his own right such as ESA or Universal Credit. (4) He is not in any apprenticeship program. (5) The claimant normally lives in the UK (Manchester), satisfying residency requirements. (6) The claimant lives with Alex full-time, establishing responsibility for the child. (7) No one else is currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex. (8) Alex lives normally at home and is not in hospital, care, or foster arrangements. (9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria have been met.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their 8-year-old son Alex. The claimant meets all UK residency requirements (living in Manchester), has responsibility for Alex (living together full-time), and Alex meets all child eligibility criteria as an 8-year-old in primary school with no disqualifying circumstances. No other person is claiming for Alex, and the local council is not contributing to his care.
